prompt: A platform owner wants a visual that immediately shows whether open P1 incidents are above or below target. Which report visualization is best?
choices: List report grouped by assignment group
Single score report
Gauge with warning and critical thresholds
Calendar report
explanation: A gauge is designed to compare a metric to target thresholds and quickly communicate status with color bands.

prompt: Which statement correctly describes a homepage in classic ServiceNow UI?
choices: It is a collection of report widgets and content blocks users can view for operational monitoring.
It is the only place where ACL rules can be configured.
It replaces update sets for migrating records.
It is required to run scripted REST APIs.
explanation: Homepages provide role-based at-a-glance views by arranging reports, gauges, and other widgets in one place.

prompt: An admin needs a chart showing incidents opened by priority during the last 30 days and refreshed daily. What should they configure first?
choices: A scheduled import set transform
A report with a date filter and grouping by priority
A business rule on sys_user
A UI policy on the incident form
explanation: The core requirement is reporting logic: define the report data source, filter window, and grouping to visualize incident volume by priority.

prompt: Why would an administrator add multiple gauges to the same homepage?
choices: To enforce ACLs on tables used by reports
To track several KPIs with separate thresholds in one operational view
To replace assignment rules for incidents
To prevent users from creating personal reports
explanation: Each gauge can represent a different KPI with distinct warning/critical limits, giving teams a compact health overview.

prompt: A manager says, "I only need one number: currently open high-priority incidents." Which report type is most appropriate?
choices: Pivot report
Trend report
Single score report
Calendar report
explanation: Single score reports are intended to display one aggregate metric, such as count of open high-priority incidents.

prompt: A developer wants logic to run once after deploying a scoped app update to backfill a new field. What is the best mechanism?
choices: Fix script
Before query business rule
Client script
Catalog UI policy
explanation: Fix scripts are intended for one-time data correction or backfill tasks during deployment or maintenance.

prompt: What is a key characteristic of a processor in ServiceNow?
choices: It defines table-level ACL inheritance.
It provides a URL endpoint that executes server-side script logic.
It renders Workspace component trees.
It replaces Scripted REST APIs in all cases.
explanation: Processors expose server-side logic through a URL endpoint and are commonly used for custom request handling patterns.

prompt: Which statement about Data Policies is correct?
choices: They only enforce field requirements on Service Portal forms.
They enforce data consistency rules on records, including data imported through non-UI channels.
They run only when a client script calls g_form.save().
They are limited to read-only behavior and cannot require fields.
explanation: Data Policies help enforce mandatory/read-only rules beyond the standard form UI, improving consistency for imported or API-sourced data.

prompt: When executing a fix script that updates many records, what should a developer do first to reduce risk?
choices: Run directly in production during peak hours
Test in sub-production, validate query scope, and review rollback approach
Disable all ACLs temporarily
Convert the script to a client-side onLoad script
explanation: Large data corrections should be validated in lower environments with precise query scope and a rollback strategy before production execution.

prompt: A team needs to reject records missing a required cost center even when loaded by import sets. Which configuration best supports this?
choices: UI Policy on the form
Client script with g_form.showFieldMsg()
Data Policy requiring the field
Formatter on the form layout
explanation: Data Policies provide server-side style enforcement that can apply to imported data, not just interactive form entry.

prompt: In ITSM, what is a primary benefit of deploying Virtual Agent for common support requests?
choices: It automatically decommissions inactive CMDB CIs.
It routes all incidents directly to Problem Management.
It deflects repetitive contacts by guiding users through conversational self-service flows.
It eliminates the need for a service catalog.
explanation: Virtual Agent reduces service desk load by handling frequent, repeatable user intents through guided conversations.

prompt: A self-service strategy emphasizes "shift-left" support. Which outcome best aligns with that goal?
choices: More tickets reassigned between resolver groups
Users resolve simple issues through knowledge or virtual assistant before contacting agents
All incidents are converted to change requests
Catalog approvals are bypassed for speed
explanation: Shift-left focuses on moving resolution closer to the user through knowledge and automation, reducing agent-handled demand.

prompt: Which design choice improves Virtual Agent success for password reset and similar ITSM requests?
choices: Use long free-text prompts with no decision buttons
Create clear intents and guided topic flows with explicit next actions
Trigger CAB approval before every conversation step
Disable handoff to live agent entirely
explanation: Well-defined intents and guided branching reduce ambiguity and make self-service conversations reliable and efficient.

prompt: Which KPI best indicates whether ITSM self-service is reducing service desk workload?
choices: Increase in incident reassignment count
Decrease in first response SLA target
Growth in successful self-service/VA resolutions with fewer agent-created incidents
Number of update sets promoted per week
explanation: The key signal is successful user resolution through self-service channels and reduced need for agent ticket creation.

prompt: When should a Virtual Agent topic hand off to a live agent in ITSM?
choices: Only after closing the user's incident
When confidence is low, user intent is ambiguous, or policy requires human approval
Immediately for every greeting
Never, because full automation is mandatory
explanation: Effective VA design includes escalation paths for low-confidence or high-judgment situations to preserve user experience and compliance.
